elements.sent-2411-001: Channels with synchronization errors, exhibiting deletion and insertion errors, find practical applications in DNA storage, data reconstruction, and various other domains.
elements.sent-2411-002: Presence of insertions and deletions render the channel with memory, complicating capacity analysis.
elements.sent-2411-003: For instance, despite the formulation of an independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) deletion channel more than fifty years ago, and proof that the channel is information stable, hence its Shannon capacity exists, calculation of the capacity remained elusive.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, a relatively recent result establishes the capacity of the deletion channel in the asymptotic regime of small deletion probabilities by computing the dominant terms of the capacity expansion.
elements.sent-2411-005: This paper extends that result to binary insertion channels, determining the dominant terms of the channel capacity for small insertion probabilities and establishing capacity in this asymptotic regime.
elements.sent-2411-007: To prove our results, we build on methods used for the deletion channel, employing Bernoulli$(1/2)$ inputs for achievability and coupling this with a converse using stationary and ergodic processes as inputs, and show that the channel capacity differs only in the higher order terms from the achievable rates with i.i.d.. inputs.
elements.sent-2411-008: The results, for instance, show that the capacity of the random insertion channel is higher than that of the Gallager insertion channel, and quantifies the difference in the asymptotic regime.
elements.sent-2411-009: Synchronization-error channels, including deletion, repetition, and insertion channels, serve as effective models for a variety of realistic and practical scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-010: Such channel models find extensive applications in DNA coding \cite{abroshan2019coding, balado2010embedding , DNA-based storage systems \cite{heckel2019characterization, lenz2019coding , magnetic data storage systems \cite{guan2014coding , data reconstruction \cite{levenshtein2001efficient , and numerous other domains.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Despite the wide range of application areas, the capacity of channels with synchronization errors remains an underexplored research area.
elements.sent-2411-012: In \cite{dobrushin1967shannon , Dobrushin establishes counterparts to Shannon's discrete memoryless channel's (DMC's) capacity formulation \cite{shannon for a wide range of channels with synchronization errors.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: He demonstrates that the channel capacity for such channels can be defined by the maximal mutual information per bit and can be achieved through a random coding scheme.
elements.sent-2411-014: In \cite{10619598, morozov2024shannon , the authors consider channels with synchronization errors governed by a Markov chain.}
elements.sent-2411-015: It is shown that if the underlying Markov chain is stationary and ergodic, the information capacity exists and is equal to the coding capacity.
elements.sent-2411-016: This result generalizes Dobrushin's coding theorem \cite{dobrushin1967shannon on the information stability of channels with memoryless synchronization errors.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Despite the early result establishing the information stability of the memoryless synchronization error channels, the calculation of the exact capacity proved extremely challenging.
elements.sent-2411-018: For instance, even for the seemingly simple channel model of independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) deletion channel, the channel capacity is unknown.
elements.sent-2411-019: As such the literature on channels with synchronization errors predominantly focuses on establishing lower and upper bounds for channel capacity.
elements.sent-2411-020: In \cite{gallager2000sequential , Gallager proposed using a pseudo-random string to convolutional codes with sequential decoding to correct synchronization and substitution errors.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Tree codes are utilized in \cite{zigangirov1969sequential to obtain lower bounds for insertion/deletion channels.}
elements.sent-2411-022: In \cite{mitzenmacher2006simple , a lower bound for the deletion channel is established by demonstrating a connection between deletion channels and the Poisson-repeat channel using jig-saw decoding.}
elements.sent-2411-023: This lower bound is further improved in \cite{kirsch2009directly for the capacity of channels with independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) deletions and duplications.}
elements.sent-2411-024: To develop upper bounds on the capacity of channels with synchronization errors, typical technique is to employ a genie-aided approach with additional side information, which results in more tractable channel models.
elements.sent-2411-025: In \cite{diggavi2007capacity , an upper bound on the deletion channel capacity is derived using undeletable markers.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Similarly, Ref. \cite{fertonani2010novel provides deletion channel upper bounds coupled with lower bounds, by using different genie-aided systems.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In \cite{rahmati2013upper , an upper bound for the non-binary deletion channel is obtained.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Furthermore, in \cite{fertonani2010bounds , capacity upper bounds are obtained for more general synchronization error channels exhibiting insertions, deletions and substitutions.}
elements.sent-2411-029: In \cite{10.1145/3281275 , using a systematic approach based on convex programming and real analysis, the author derives upper bounds on the capacity of the binary deletion channel, channels with i.i.d.}
elements.sent-2411-030: This framework can be applied to obtain capacity upper bounds for any repetition distribution.
elements.sent-2411-031: A recent and comprehensive overview of the capacity results of synchronization-error channels is provided in \cite{cheraghchi2020overview .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Parallel to the results establishing upper and lower bounds on the channel capacity, there have also been efforts determining the behavior of the capacity of the deletion channel in the asymptotic regime of small deletion probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-033: For instance, \cite{5513746 demonstrates that the capacity of the deletion channel is comparable to that of the binary symmetric error channel in the asymptotic case for small deletion probabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The authors establish that the trivial capacity lower bound for this channel, given by $1 - H(p)$, is tight in the limit.
elements.sent-2411-035: They provide an upper bound of $1 - (1 - o(1))H(p)$, where the $o(1)$ term vanishes as the deletion probability $p \rightarrow 0$.
elements.sent-2411-036: More in line with our work in this paper, the works presented in \cite{kanoria2010deletion, kanoria2013optimal leverage Dobrushin’s coding theorem \cite{dobrushin1967shannon to approximate the capacity of the binary deletion channel using series expansions up to the second and third leading terms, respectively, for small deletion probabilities.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: These studies establish the channel’s capacity in the asymptotic regime of small deletion probabilities by calculating the dominant terms of the capacity expansion.
elements.sent-2411-038: Additionally, they demonstrate that the optimal input distribution can be obtained by perturbing the i.i.d.. Bernoulli$(1/2)$ input distribution.
elements.sent-2411-039: In \cite{6457365 , a similar approach is applied to duplication channels, where the channel capacity is computed for small values of the duplication probability using a series expansion.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Note that, although the duplication channel is closely related to the insertion channel and shares similarities with our channel model, it duplicates existing bits/symbols rather than inserting random bits/symbols, hence no runs are lost or no new runs are created, significantly simplifying the capacity calculation.

elements.sent-2411-042: In this paper, we adopt a similar systematic approach to insertion channels, which serve as practical models for certain real-world communication schemes including DNA storage.
elements.sent-2411-043: We consider two fundamental binary insertion channel models: 1) the simple insertion channel, in which a random bit is inserted after each bit with a certain probability, and 2) the Gallager insertion channel, in which each bit is replaced with two random bits with a certain probability.
elements.sent-2411-044: In both cases, we focus on small insertion probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-045: We explore the first two terms in the series expansion of these channel models' capacities and establish their achievability using i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-046: Bernoulli$(1/2)$ input distribution, and establish a matching converse for the dominant (lower order) terms.
elements.sent-2411-047: Our main results are as follows: let $C(\alpha)$ be the capacity of an insertion channel with insertion probability $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Then, for the simple insertion channel model with small $\alpha$ and any $\epsilon>0$, we have \begin{equation}C(\alpha) = 1 + \alpha \log(\alpha) +G_1\alpha + O(\alpha^{{\color{black}{3/2 - \epsilon}}}),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}G_1 = - \log(e) + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{l=1}^{\infty} 2^{-l-1}l \log l \frac{1}{2}\sum_{a = 1}^\infty \sum_{b = 1}^\infty (b+1)2^{-a}2^{-b}h\left(\frac{1}{b+1} \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-049: For the Gallager insertion channel, we obtain \begin{equation}C(\alpha) = 1 + \alpha \log(\alpha) +G_2\alpha + O(^{{\color{black}{3/2 - \epsilon}}}),\end{equation} with \begin{equation}G_2 = - \log(e) - \frac{7}{8} +\frac{1}{4} \sum_{l=1}^{\infty} 2^{-l-1}l \log l + \frac{1}{4} \sum_{a = 1}^\infty \sum_{b = 1}^\infty (a+b+2)2^{-a}2^{-b}h\left(\frac{a+1}{a+b+2} \right),\end{equation} where $G_1 \approx 0.4901$, and $G_2 \approx -0.5865$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-050: Approximations obtained by omitting the terms with higher order for these channel models are illustrated in Fig. \ref{f2 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: As may be anticipated, the Gallager insertion channel replaces a bit with two random bits; hence, for a specific input, the ambiguity of the channel output is higher compared to the simple insertion channel model with random bit insertion (without replacement).

elements.section-2411-004: Capacity Approximation for the Simple Insertion Channel Model
elements.sent-2411-096: In this section, we provide a capacity approximation for the first insertion channel model (dubbed the simple insertion channel) by computing the first two terms of the series expansion for small insertion probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-097: Our main result is given as follows.
elements.sent-2411-098: Let $C(\alpha)$ be the capacity of the simple insertion channel with insertion probability $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-099: Then, for small $\alpha$ and any $\epsilon > 0$, we have \begin{equation}C(\alpha) = 1 + \alpha \log(\alpha) +G_1\alpha + O(\alpha^{3/2-\epsilon})\end{equation} where \begin{equation}G_1 = -\log(e) + + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{l=1}^{\infty} 2^{-l-1}l \log l + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{a = 1}^\infty \sum_{b = 1}^\infty (b+1)2^{-a}2^{-b}h\left(\frac{1}{b+1} \right) = -\log(e) + \frac{1}{2}\sum_{l=1}^{\infty} 2^{-l-1}l \log l + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{a = 1}^\infty \sum_{b = 1}^\infty (b+1)2^{-a}2^{-b}h\left(\frac{1}{b+1} \right),\end{equation} which is approximated as $G_1 \approx 0.4901$.
elements.sent-2411-100: To establish this approximation, the first step involves calculating the terms in $I(X^n, Y(X^n))$, as explicitly given in Section \ref{sec:IXnYn and provided in \eqref{cap2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-101: We elaborate on these calculations in the subsequent subsections.
elements.sent-2411-102: We define the process denoting the insertion patterns corresponding to the sequence of insertion channels as $\mathbb{A}$, which is an i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-103: Bernoulli($\alpha$) process that determines the locations of insertions, independent of the input.
elements.sent-2411-104: With this process and channel model, we represent the corresponding $n$-bit binary vector as $A^n$.
elements.sent-2411-105: Each element of $A^n$, denoted as $A_i$, is set to `1' if there is an insertion after the $i$-th bit of the input vector, and `0' otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-106: For the inserted bits, as described in Section \ref{sec:prelim , $B^i = 0$ if there is no insertion after the $i$-th bit while $B_i \sim \text{Ber (1/2)$ otherwise.}}

elements.sent-2411-108: To compute $H(A^n,B^n)$ in \eqref{cap2 , one can use the independence of the bits: \begin{equation}\label{hdb} H(A^n,B^n) = \sum_{i=1}^n H(A_i, B_i).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Note that the joint probability mass function (PMF) of $A_i$ and $B_i$ can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{probs} P(A_i = a, B_i = b)=  1-\alpha, if  (a,b) = (0,0) \frac{\alpha}{2}, if  (a,b) = (1,0) or  (a,b) = (1,1) 0, otherwise. \end{equation} As a result, we have \begin{equation}H(A_i, B_i) = -(1-\alpha)\log(1-\alpha)-\alpha \log(\alpha/2) \nonumber = -(1-\alpha)\log(1-\alpha)-\alpha\log(\alpha)+\alpha \nonumber = h(\alpha) + \alpha,\end{equation} where $h(\alpha)$ is the binary entropy function.

elements.section-2411-005: Capacity Approximation for the Gallager Insertion Channel
elements.sent-2411-280: In this section, we provide the capacity approximation for the Gallager insertion channel model, similar to the technique used for the first insertion channel model.
elements.sent-2411-281: The main result for the capacity is presented in the following theorem.
elements.sent-2411-282: Let $C(\alpha)$ be the capacity of the insertion channel with insertion probability $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-283: Then, for small $\alpha$ and any $\epsilon > 0$, we have \begin{equation}C(\alpha) = 1 + \alpha \log(\alpha) +G_2 \alpha + {\color{black}{ O(\alpha^{3/2-\epsilon})}},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}G_2 = - \log(e) - \frac{7}{8} +\frac{1}{4} \sum_{l=1}^{\infty} 2^{-l-1}l \log l \nonumber + {\color{black}{\frac{1}{4}}}\sum_{a = 1}^\infty \sum_{b = 1}^\infty (a+b+2)2^{-a}2^{-b}h\left(\frac{a+1}{a+b+2} \right),\end{equation} which is approximated as $G_2 \approx -0.5865$.

elements.sent-2411-373: In this paper, we focused on the capacity of insertion channels with small insertion probabilities, a topic that has been largely overlooked in the literature despite its relevance in practical applications such as DNA coding and storage.
elements.sent-2411-374: Our approach builds upon the series expansion of a previously known capacity point, specifically a channel with no insertions (i.e., zero insertion probability), where the capacity is 1 and is achieved with an i.i.d.. Bernoulli$(1/2)$ input distribution.
elements.sent-2411-375: By leveraging this known point, we expect that for small insertion probabilities, the achievable rate will not deviate significantly from this value.
elements.sent-2411-376: With this intuition, we derived the first two terms of the capacity expansion by decomposing the expressions necessary to achieve the maximum rate.
elements.sent-2411-377: Using this methodology, we obtained capacity approximations for two fundamental insertion channel models: 1) the insertion channel with random bit insertions, and 2) the Gallager insertion channel, where a bit is replaced by two random bits.
elements.sent-2411-378: Our results demonstrate that these approximations are achievable by a uniform i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-379: For future work, possible extensions include applying similar methods to different channel models or symbol alphabets.
elements.sent-2411-380: For instance, the binary input case could be extended to nonbinary inputs, e.g., 4-ary for DNA storage systems, with deletion and/or insertion channels.
elements.sent-2411-381: Further research could also consider different channel models, such as the absorption channel or the insertion, deletion, and substitution (IDS) channel.
elements.sent-2411-382: Thus, our approach lays the foundation for various extensions and can facilitate capacity analysis for these different models.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
